title: What is "Slop" (and why it gives me hope)
summary: Hank tries to define "slop," arguing it isn't simply AI or low effort, since AI used for utility (like cleaning up audio or writing code) rarely draws backlash. Instead, he proposes an almost economic definition: in a world of near-infinite content, anything so easy to make that it floods the space quickly loses value, both because abundance lowers worth and because our pattern-matching brains devalue the familiar. He concludes slop is content whose ease of creation destroys its own value, using AI cat videos and Fruit Love Island as test cases.

title: My Big 2026 Prediction is...Not Fun
summary: Hank makes his big 2026 prediction: artificial intelligence could fracture Trump's coalition, which currently unites grievance-based populists with billionaire elites through Trump's promise that he's "greedy and mean for you." He argues AI data centers are boxes that eat jobs and concentrate capital while raising power bills, which is fundamentally not a populist vibe. Whether the AI bubble pops (forcing unpopular bailouts) or succeeds (eliminating jobs), both outcomes strain a coalition that only holds as long as people don't suffer visible consequences. He also promotes the Nerdfighteria census.

title: The Web is Going to Die
summary: From the preserved ghost town of Garnet, Montana, Hank observes that abandoned towns are actually rare because people and places bond through shared infrastructure and network effects, so most towns build atop their old skeletons rather than freezing in time. He draws a parallel to dead social platforms like Vine, MySPace, and GeoCities, arguing the digital world produces ghost towns constantly because leaving is so easy. He worries the decentralized web is dying into a place we visit to remember what life used to be, and that even thriving platforms will someday be abandoned.

title: I've Not Been Doing Well
summary: John candidly admits he hasn't been well, describing how he increasingly treats himself as a tool to maximize impact, which makes him more capable but less happy. He recounts recent stress, exhaustion, aches, and headaches that prompted cancer-relapse testing, which came back clear (still in remission), leaving anxiety, possible long COVID, or aging as explanations. Citing a moment from his novel where a character insists problems get solved by other people, he wrestles with letting go of the belief that he alone must do the work, and looks forward to Pizza Miss as restorative "work nostalgia."

title: This Might Actually be Happening
summary: Avoiding the overwhelming present, Hank explains NASA's Perseverance discovery in Jezero Crater: colored spots in a mudstone containing vivianite and greigite, where iron and sulfur appear to have dropped from stable high-energy states to lower ones without the heat that would normally cause it. Since Earth microbes like Geobacter and sulfate-reducing bacteria harvest exactly this kind of energy, the finding is a potential biosignature. He stresses this means "maybe past life," not proof, and uses the history of Viking and the ALH84001 meteorite to show science advances through claims, counter-explanations, and revision rather than single decisive experiments.

title: The Problem with My Brother's Hit New App
summary: John celebrates Hank's app Focus Friend reaching number one in the US, describing its mechanic where a knitting bean gets distracted when you use your phone, which has helped John write instead of scrolling. His comedic complaint is the in-app-purchase "John Bean," which depicts him as fully gray-haired when he insists he has brown hair with a bit of silver, tying it to his anxiety about aging and mortality. He affectionately marvels at Hank's relentless inventiveness while joking that each new venture gives him more gray hairs.

title: What #1 New York Times Bestseller Actually Means
summary: John demystifies what "#1 New York Times bestseller" actually means, explaining that Everything Is Tuberculosis topped the nonfiction list largely because massive pre-orders and 100,000 signed copies guaranteed it, and that the ranking reflects sales relative to category, not absolute numbers. He notes there are many simultaneous #1 bestsellers across lists, that 85% of traditionally published books sell under 5,000 copies, and that bestseller status isn't always life-changing money (Paper Towns earned about $105,000 over three years). He urges readers to seek recommendations from librarians and booksellers and read beyond the bestseller lists.

title: You’re Not Addicted to Content, You’re Starving for Information
summary: Responding to John's video comparing the social internet to cigarettes, Hank argues that metaphor fails because cigarettes offer no benefit while information and content genuinely can. He proposes food as a better analogy: humanity moved from calorie scarcity to ultra-abundance, and companies now engineer hyper-palatable, cheap food that hacks our brains, mirroring engagement-optimized content. He notes that giving people information (calorie counts) didn't fix overeating, and that solutions require cultural change, reduced agency-awareness (the elephant-and-rider metaphor), and time, suggesting we're information-starved amid junk rather than simply addicted.

title: What Trump's Doing
summary: John argues that Trump's attempt to unilaterally dissolve USAID isn't really about the agency but about whether the Constitution still matters, since Congress holds the power of the purse and created USAID by law, making its dismantling by executive action illegal. Citing Ezra Klein's line that "Trump is acting like a king because he is too weak to govern like a president," he frames the flurry of executive orders as flooding the zone or avoiding legislative failure. He insists the Constitution's authority rests on collective belief in separation of powers, and hopes courts and citizens affirm it rather than merely tolerate the attempts.

title: This Is Not Normal
summary: John shares his most encouraging fact: global child mortality has fallen over 50% since he finished high school in 1995, from 11 million to under 5 million under-five deaths, part of a historically unprecedented reversal of a problem that plagued all prior human history. He stresses this progress was not natural or inevitable but earned through sanitation, medicine, reduced poverty, and government programs like PEPFAR and the Global Fund. He is heartbroken that the new administration's freeze on foreign aid threatens TB and HIV treatment, warning that interrupted treatment breeds drug resistance and that continued progress depends entirely on human choices.

title: Do Audiobooks Count As Reading?
summary: Recording the Everything Is Tuberculosis audiobook, John reflects on how his once-snobbish view that audiobooks aren't "real" reading has completely reversed. He argues the point of reading is to ingest words and ideas as transparently as possible, and since research shows most people actually retain more from audio, audiobooks are the ideal form of reading for many. He notes each format has unique strengths (lingering on text versus hearing inflection), that "reluctant readers" are often voracious audio readers, and that both formats reach the deep places books are meant to reach, so both count.

title: Fine, I'll Talk About the Drones...
summary: Hank examines the 2024 New Jersey drone panic, arguing most sightings were misidentifications rather than something genuinely weird. He explains drones evade radar simply because they fly low and small, and that humans can't judge the size or distance of lights in the night sky beyond about 20 feet without reference objects, so "car-sized drones" became widely reported despite little actual evidence. He shows examples that turned out to be planes landing, contrails, and stars (as Larry Hogan tweeted), and stresses that priming makes people see what they expect, while officials dismissively telling worried citizens they're imagining things only erodes trust.

title: Populism, Media Revolutions, and Our Terrible Moment
summary: Hank argues we're living through a media revolution as disruptive as any in 500 years. He uses 1930s radio to show how a new, magical, unregulated medium let charismatic figures like Father Coughlin speak directly to millions, fueling a rise in populism, which he defines not as an ideology but as a marketing strategy that names people's grievances and an enemy. He distinguishes radio and TV as centralizing forces (easily regulated, finite) from the decentralizing internet, which he likens instead to Gutenberg's printing press, framing Trump as a product of this recurring post-media-revolution populist pattern.

title: These Maps are Lying (if you let them)
summary: Hank explains how election maps and graphs mislead, starting with a viral chart that used both wrong data (uncounted slow-counting California ballots) and a non-zero axis to imply Biden's 2020 votes were fake. He argues standard red-blue maps lie because "land does not vote," making cities look insignificant, and that popular vote-swing maps show only the change in partisan margin, not absolute vote counts, while county-based sizing distorts tiny counties like Loving County, Texas (96 voters) against LA County. He frames these as useful simplifying tools that carry real risk of teaching the wrong lessons.

title: My Ballot Made Me Angry
summary: Hank recounts opening his Montana mail ballot and being angered that Democrats were listed last in all three federal races, suspecting manipulation since ballot-order effects can shift results by up to 5%. Investigating the code on his ballot, he discovers Montana rotates candidate order by district so every candidate appears on top an equal number of times, restoring some faith. He uses this to argue that "everything is a conspiracy when you don't trust anything," and that fairness like this exists only because people identified an unfair system, confirmed it with research, and fixed it through policy.

title: Is Google Training AI on YouTube Videos?
summary: Hank examines reporting showing generative AI companies trained on YouTube videos and transcripts without permission, with his own channels heavily represented. He argues creators feel ripped off because big rights-holders got paid via licensing deals while independents did not. Analyzing a Bloomberg interview with YouTube's CEO, he concludes Google treats the standard upload license as sufficient legal basis to train Gemini, even though that license never explicitly mentions AI training.

title: Three Whole Onions with Secretary Pete Buttigieg
summary: Hank films a dual interview with Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg at Missoula's new airport. They cover who owns and funds airports (usually local government plus a ~$4.50 passenger charge) and Southern states trying to seize control of city-run airports amid partisan tension. Buttigieg explains how taxiway design reduces ground-collision risk, argues parking minimums waste land, and says autonomous vehicles could cut the ~40,000 annual traffic deaths, though Hank doubts people will give up having cars ready.

title: I Believed These Four Lies
summary: Hank recounts four things he wrongly believed. A viral rent-vs-income graph diverged mainly because income was inflation-adjusted but rent wasn't. A claim that Jane Goodall wanted to cut the population to 500 million misattributed an implication she never stated. A John Stewart clip citing an 8% US tax burden secretly meant only federal income tax for a specific family during pandemic aid. And a NOAA disaster-cost graph reflects more buildings built since 1980, not purely climate change. His theme: people, himself included, project conclusions onto data.

title: I Quit
summary: Hank announces that after cancer treatment he is permanently stepping back from being CEO of DFTBA and Complexly rather than returning full-time. He explains he was never truly full-time and relied on leaders like Dave and Julie, and that succession planning began before his diagnosis. He reflects on the fear of letting go and the worry a successor might decide differently, but is relieved new CEO LJ Dukovski embraces the companies' weirdness. Julie becomes acting CEO of Complexly; Hank shifts to special projects.

title: The Weirdest Post-Chemo Symptoms
summary: Hank describes surprising side effects of finishing chemotherapy. He can no longer donate blood, his hair 're-rolled' unpredictably in texture and color, and he now studies other men's beards. He muses that culture is a kind of collective posthumous 'posting,' since words come from forgotten people. He also details ocular migraines and psychological aftereffects: health anxiety triggered by work anxiety, and survivor's guilt from feeling his cancer 'didn't hurt him enough' to warrant discussing it.

title: The Biggest Science Story of the Week
summary: Hank argues the biggest under-covered science story is why North Atlantic temperatures spiked. A 2020 UN rule requiring low-sulfur ship fuel cut sulfur dioxide, which had seeded cooling 'ship track' clouds, so removing that pollution unmasked additional warming. He frames this as an accidental, priceless geoengineering experiment. He contends deliberate geoengineering (like spraying seawater to seed clouds) is coming, and humanity must do three things at once: stop emitting CO2, remove old CO2, and manage current warming's deadly impacts.

title: The Good News Video
summary: Hank shares two pieces of good news. First, after John's video and nerdfighter pressure, Johnson & Johnson granted a license letting generic bedaquiline reach most low- and middle-income countries, removing the TB drug's patent barrier within days. Second, his Hodgkin lymphoma responded well to just four chemo sessions; PET scans show most swollen lymph nodes shrank to normal. He'll do radiation to clear remaining cells and expects remission in about two months, expressing gratitude for accessible modern treatment.

title: Barely Contained Rage: An Open Letter to Johnson & Johnson
summary: John writes an open letter urging Johnson & Johnson not to enforce a secondary 'evergreening' patent on the TB drug bedaquiline, which he says would deny access to roughly 6 million people over four years. He explains TB is the world's deadliest infectious disease, that drug development stalled once it stopped affecting rich countries, and that the secondary patent is like patenting a 'pen cap.' Since public money largely funded the drug, he argues it should return to the public, and asks nerdfighteria to pressure J&J to honor its patients-first credo.

title: What to Get for a Cancer Patient
summary: Drawing on his cancer experience and thousands of Twitter replies, Hank advises how to support sick people. Rather than the burdensome 'let me know what I can do,' offer specific concrete things. The most-appreciated gifts were meal-delivery and gas gift cards, meals in disposable containers, and comfort items implying rest. Avoid funeral flowers and schedule check-ins for months later when support fades. Surprisingly, what people wanted most was simply time and company. He warns against medical advice unless you've had the same treatment.

title: Alone on the Cancer Boat: A Vlogbrothers Reunion
summary: John visits and the brothers film a candid conversation during Hank's chemo. Hank uses an extended 'alone on a boat' metaphor for cancer: seasick on a boat you can't leave while supporters on shore can't get you off. They discuss how cancer isn't one disease but many, why it proved far harder to cure than infectious diseases, and the shocking global inequality in treatment access. They riff on micromorts, ocular migraines, and family, blending humor with the reality of serious illness.

title: Understanding Cancer Survival Rates
summary: Hank explains why cancer survival rates are more complicated than they seem. A raw death rate hides crucial detail: when deaths occur, how old the treatments were, and how varied patients are. He defends the five-year survival rate because surviving five years often predicts longer survival, longer windows rely on outdated data, and it's a clear quick statistic. He stresses that different cancers, stages, and grades are essentially different diseases, so broad statistics apply poorly, and points to disease-free and progression-free survival as richer measures.

title: Rough Week
summary: John addresses Hank as chemo begins, expressing love and reassuring him he isn't alone. He reflects on how humor and grief coexist in illness, sharing funny moments while distinguishing humor rooted in love and inclusion from humor that judges or stigmatizes. He invokes Partners in Health's idea of 'accompaniment', walking someone's path with them, offers a prayer, and thanks the 100-plus employees now supporting the companies under his temporary, self-deprecating 'Kirkland brand Hank Green' leadership.

title: So, I've got cancer
summary: Hank announces his diagnosis of Hodgkin lymphoma after enlarged lymph nodes led to a quick ultrasound and biopsy. He notes the unusual speed of US healthcare signaled seriousness, but shares that Hodgkin is among the most treatable cancers with cure as the goal. He explains lymphoma is a blood cancer already throughout the body, so spread matters less than with solid tumors. He resolves to treat cancer as his 'job,' take it one day at a time, make videos only when he feels able, and let go of what he can't control.

title: Elon Was Right
summary: Hank concedes that while he was right that content moderation is a social platform's core product, Elon Musk was right about something he missed. Musk's audience genuinely worries about 'free speech' meaning distrust of unknown people making moderation decisions; it was never about policies but about who makes them, about vibes. Hank predicted the audience would sour when Musk still had to moderate, but was wrong. People flock to Twitter for erratic, reality-TV drama, though he doubts that antagonistic, Elon-fan-club model is sustainable.

title: This "Math Test" Changed How I See Humanity
summary: Hank uses a viral ambiguous math problem (where '3' could be 12 or 18 depending on the unstated rule) to explain how the social internet drives conflict. The problem is genuinely poorly written, so the only correct stance is knowing you can't know, yet people argue confidently. He observes math goes viral only when people fight, and that vague questions, easy to form conflicting simple opinions about, are exactly what algorithms surface. These divisive, ill-posed questions drive wedges not just between parties but within communities that mostly agree.

title: Most People Have Never Been 20
summary: John investigates when the median human of the roughly 117 billion people who have ever lived was born, and finds the surprising answer is around 10-100 CE, about 2,000 years ago. This isn't because there were many people then but because child mortality was catastrophically high: when the median person lived, about half of all people died before age 10. He corrects the myth that low life expectancy meant adults died at 30, arguing it reflected child death, and closes by noting we can choose a world where fewer die early, as Partners in Health works toward.

title: Here's Why Younger People are Getting Shingles
summary: Hank explains why viruses aren't named after the diseases they cause, using the varicella zoster virus, which causes both chickenpox in children and shingles later in life when the dormant virus reactivates in nerves. He argues that mass chickenpox vaccination since 1995 in the US has removed the natural 'booster' that exposure to sick kids once gave adults' immune systems, so younger people like him (aided by stress and immunosuppressants) are now getting shingles earlier. He frames it as shingles rates rising before they fall long-term, and honors Evelyn Nicol, who isolated the virus and later died of COVID-19.

title: The Biggest Climate Bill of Your Life - But What does it DO!?
summary: Hank breaks down what the major US climate bill actually does, arguing that its large-scale investments in clean energy and infrastructure will shape the country for decades in ways that will be largely invisible to most people even as they reshape emissions and industry.

title: My Religion and My Banned Books
summary: Responding to his books being banned for supposed obscenity, John reflects on his time as a hospital chaplain, where he witnessed truly obscene things like children dying and families crushed by medical bills. He explains that his job there was not to fix pain but to listen and acknowledge others' full humanity, and that his books aim to do the same: to 'stand there' and invite readers into other lives. He argues great books have a 'humanizing agenda,' and that book banning is often an effort to restrict empathy, which conflicts with his Christian call to treat all others as he'd want to be treated.

title: Ukraine and Russia: What Caused the War?
summary: John gives historical context for Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, tracing Ukraine's contested statehood from the 1917 Ukrainian People's Republic through Soviet conquest. He highlights two lasting Stalin-era policies: the Holodomor terror-famine that killed perhaps one in eight Ukrainians, and forced deportations that reshaped the region's ethnic makeup. He argues the war is fundamentally about Putin's stated belief that Ukraine is not a legitimate nation and should exist only in partnership with Russia, dismissing NATO and Russian-speaker-protection justifications, and insists Ukraine has the right to exist freely.

title: The Amazon isn't "Burning" - It's Being Burned
summary: Hank argues the headline should be that the Amazon is 'being burned,' not 'burning,' because unlike US wildfires, fire is not natural to the rainforest and the 2019 fires were deliberately set to clear land for cattle and farming. He blames the surge on President Bolsonaro rolling back environmental protections and lying about deforestation data, driven by a nationalist ideology that treats global concern for the Amazon as a threat to sovereignty. He explains the rainforest makes its own rain via evapotranspiration, so destroying it risks a feedback loop that would devastate Brazil's own climate and agriculture, and stresses the need to call lies lies.

title: What /Actually/ Happened at Chernobyl
summary: Hank explains the physics behind the Chernobyl disaster, defining fission, radioactive decay, isotopes, and how neutron moderators speed up reactions while absorbers slow them. He describes how the cheap RBMK reactor used graphite moderators and ordinary water (a neutron absorber) as coolant, creating dangerous positive feedback. On the accident day, a buildup of neutron-absorbing xenon-135 sank the reactor's power, so operators withdrew nearly all control rods to force it back up; the graphite tips on those rods then briefly accelerated the reaction as they re-entered, contributing to the explosion.

title: We just had our first interstellar visitor...and it's weird.
summary: Hank discusses 'Oumuamua, the first observed interstellar object, describing its high speed, dramatic brightness variations suggesting an extreme elongated shape, and a non-gravitational acceleration. He lays out the two leading explanations: it off-gassed like a comet, or it's a thin, reflective solar sail pushed by photon momentum, an idea appealing to science fiction fans. He candidly admits he wants it to be alien technology and therefore can't fully trust his own judgment, using it as a lesson in how the appeal of an idea shouldn't sway scientific evaluation, while noting better telescopes are coming.

title: Why is the Disney "D" So Weird?
summary: Hank investigates why the Disney logo's 'D' looks so strange that the word reads almost like 'Gisnep.' He debunks the idea that it's simply Walt's signature, showing Walt had many varying signatures, none with the loopy D, and that Walt Disney Pictures had no consistent logo for nearly 50 years, seemingly to project a trusted person rather than a corporation. He traces the loopy D's emergence to the early 1980s under CEO Michael Eisner and the home-video division, concluding that someone deliberately exaggerated the D into a barely-legible spiral, and that we'll likely never know exactly who or why.

title: What Everyone Gets Wrong About Global Warming
summary: Hank argues that climate change should be understood through probability distributions, not individual events. Using major hurricanes per year, he shows that any single year's storm count is one 'dot' collapsing out of a probability curve, so no single year is a trend. Global warming works by shifting that entire curve toward stronger, wetter storms, making extreme years less rare rather than guaranteed, which is why scientists use computer models to re-run seasons and why old '100-year floods' become more frequent. He urges thinking about the curve, not the dots.

title: What OCD Is Like (for Me)
summary: John describes his personal experience of OCD, noting that inner psychic pain is hard to convey except through metaphor. He explains that popular images of OCD as handwashing or neatness miss that the 'obsessive' comes first: intrusive thought spirals hijack his consciousness for days or weeks, and compulsions like repeatedly checking food for contamination are attempts to quiet those fears. He reflects on the terror of not feeling like the captain of his own mind, rejects the romanticization of mental illness as a superpower, ties it to writing 'Turtles All the Way Down,' and urges viewers to seek help.

title: On Condom Failure
summary: John unpacks the many ways the '2% condom failure rate' statistic is misunderstood. He clarifies it refers to unintended pregnancy, not disease, and is measured per year of perfect use, not per act, so over many years cumulative odds rise substantially (roughly one in three over twenty years). He distinguishes perfect use from typical use, where condoms fail about 20% of years, explaining why long-acting methods like IUDs and implants, whose typical use equals perfect use, cut unwanted pregnancies. He laments the poor sex education that left him confused.

title: The Paris Accord: What is it? And What Does it All Mean?
summary: Hank explains the Paris climate agreement, starting with how ancient plants became fossil fuels and how burning them re-releases CO2 that traps heat and destabilizes the climate we rely on for farming and coastal cities. He describes the 2015 agreement as countries collectively setting emissions goals to keep warming below 2 degrees Celsius, with wealthy nations aiding poorer ones, but with no enforcement or penalties. He argues that even unenforceable pledges are a major step that had been working, and criticizes the US withdrawal as shirking responsibility that weakens the whole effort.

title: Some Rough Advice for the "Real World"
summary: In a commencement-style talk, Hank tells graduates that the paralyzing freedom they feel after a lifetime of following instructions is normal. He argues that dream jobs come from finding a salable skill you enjoy and getting good enough that people pay you, but that most people won't find that, and that's okay because careers, diplomas, and jobs are brand-new cultural constructions unsuited to human nature. He warns against basing self-worth on them, and advises following any path, changing direction when unhappy, since the one thing you make every day is yourself.

title: The Adpocalypse: What it Means
summary: Hank explains the 2017 'Adpocalypse,' noting he dislikes advertising yet built a business on YouTube ad revenue because YouTube uniquely offers both a massive audience and a way to turn views into money. He points out that YouTube ads pay creators far less than TV, then describes how brands pulled ads after reports of them running on vile videos, tanking all creators' revenue and Google's valuation. YouTube responded by tightening its ad-eligibility algorithms, squeezing 'good but not family-friendly' channels, and Hank predicts creators will shift toward subscriptions, Patreon, and direct brand relationships as ad value erodes.

title: Trump's Budget Explained
summary: Hank breaks down Trump's proposed 2017 budget using blocks that each represent one billion dollars, focusing on the 27% of spending that is discretionary. He shows the plan boosting the military by about 52 billion (a 10% increase) plus Homeland Security and border-wall funding, paid for by deep cuts to the State Department and international aid, the EPA (hardest hit at 30%), the NIH, education, agriculture, and anti-poverty programs. He notes the entire NEA, NEH, and public broadcasting fit in a single block that would be eliminated, and predicts Congress will heavily contest it.

title: Do I Have ADHD?
summary: Hank reflects on his childhood diagnosis of a sensory processing disorder and wonders whether he'd be diagnosed with ADHD today, describing traits like hyperfocus, rage when forced to disengage, chronic lateness, and constant restlessness. He argues disorders only count as disorders when they cause ongoing impairment, and that he's less impaired now thanks to family support, favorable circumstances, and economic luck, notably getting a computer in the 1980s. He distinguishes two coping strategies, changing the environment versus improving the brain via medication or mindfulness, and stresses that no one should face understanding their own mind alone.

title: Understanding Trump's Executive Order on Immigration
summary: John examines the actual text of Trump's 2017 executive order on immigration, explaining its 90-day ban on entry from seven nations and its confusing, contradictory treatment of green-card holders and dual citizens. He argues it is poorly targeted, noting no nationals from those countries have killed an American in a terror attack and that most US attacks come from citizens or residents, citing even conservative critics like the Cato Institute and John McCain. He also details the suspension of all refugee admissions, the indefinite Syrian ban, and the religious-preference clause, concluding the order is a legally incoherent 'hot mess' that likely violates existing asylum law and sends a damaging message.

title: Why We Left Buzzfeed (Some Thoughts on Taking Risks)
summary: Gaby and Allison of Just Between Us explain why they left BuzzFeed after a year, describing it as one of the scariest decisions they'd made. They recount how the early exciting environment shifted when there was pushback about their own channel, and how they realized a company owns everything you create, meaning creators can lose their characters and series forever. Their core message: a company is never your friend, jobs can be left, and you should trust your gut and take risks even though striking out on your own means constant stress rather than roses.

title: My Post-Election Thoughts
summary: John responds to the 2016 election, acknowledging Trump won the electoral college despite Clinton's popular-vote lead and the devastation many viewers feel, especially from attacks on people's race, gender, and religion. He apologizes for how hateful discourse and sealed-off echo chambers, including the nerdfighter community, have made opposing views unfathomable, and worries the architecture of the social internet may make constructive listening impossible. He turns to hope, quoting Saladin Ahmed, Lin-Manuel Miranda, and Emily Dickinson, arguing the nation is bigger than its leaders and that ordinary people have historically bent the arc toward justice.

title: Will the U.S. Presidential Election Be Rigged?
summary: John argues the US presidential election cannot be meaningfully rigged because the electoral college creates thousands of separately administered, decentralized elections. Revisiting 2004 Ohio, he debunks fraud claims: no votes were switched, purges are legally required, and the Democratic Party's own investigation found Kerry didn't win, though the election was poorly run. He distinguishes real voter suppression from the near-nonexistent in-person fraud (31 substantiated cases in 14 years versus 36 lightning deaths in one year) and warns that a candidate loudly and falsely claiming the election is rigged dangerously erodes trust in legitimate results.

title: Understanding Donald Trump and Hillary Clinton's Tax Plans
summary: John explains the current progressive tax system using three hypothetical families, showing how marginal brackets, deductions, exemptions, and child credits mean a median household pays roughly 1% effective. He details Clinton's plan, which largely keeps the code but raises rates on income over $5 million, imposes a 30% minimum on millionaires, closes the carried-interest loophole, and expands the child credit, adding about $200 billion to debt. Trump's plan cuts to three brackets and slashes corporate rates, concentrating benefits on the wealthy while raising taxes for some larger single-parent families, and he stresses that tax cuts do not pay for themselves, projecting a multi-trillion-dollar revenue loss.

title: Failing to Follow Up The Fault in Our Stars
summary: John candidly discusses his years-long inability to publish a book after The Fault in Our Stars, whose massive success coincided with private creative failures. He recounts abandoning a teenage-identity-thieves thriller, then a storm-drain story, then a con-artists book, sobbing over hundreds of unusable pages, as intense pressure and the feeling of becoming 'the person who wrote that one book' made writing miserable. He explains he eventually stepped back to Crash Course and other work, later returning to write just for fun for Sarah, and concludes that the real change is becoming okay with not knowing whether he'll ever finish or whether people will like it.

title: Why I Didn't Vote (And Six Reasons You Might Not)
summary: John responds to six reasons eligible Americans gave for not voting in 2016. He counters that choosing the lesser of two evils is still a real choice, that down-ballot races profoundly shape communities, that getting informed via sample ballots and Ballotpedia is easy, and that minority votes in lopsided states have a moderating effect. He argues protest non-voting looks identical to apathy and that money doesn't negate one's vote (99% of millionaires vote), and shares his own shame at failing to register when he moved to Chicago in 2000, urging viewers to vote even when it's hard.

title: Understanding the Tax Plans of Donald Trump and Ted Cruz
summary: John analyzes the tax plans of Ted Cruz and Donald Trump after a federal-budget overview. Cruz would collapse seven brackets into a flat 10% income tax, raise the standard deduction, and replace corporate and payroll taxes with a VAT-style business tax that economists find appealing, but which the Tax Policy Center estimates would cut revenue by $8.6 trillion over a decade. Trump would use four brackets topping at 25% and cut corporate rates, costing an even larger $10-12 trillion; John stresses both plans lower everyone's taxes while overwhelmingly benefiting the rich and would balloon deficits, noting Trump's claim of revenue-neutrality is false.

title: Clinton vs. Sanders: POLICY IS EXCITING!
summary: Hank enthusiastically breaks down how US taxes work, explaining marginal income brackets, payroll taxes (including the Social Security cap around $118,000), and capital gains. He contrasts Clinton, who mostly keeps the code the same while adding one bracket above $5 million, with Sanders, who raises taxes across the board, adds payroll taxes to fund single-payer healthcare, and reinstates Social Security tax above $250,000. He emphasizes Sanders' bigger changes aim to trade higher taxes for lower healthcare and college costs, and honestly concludes that experts disagree and he genuinely doesn't know which plan is best.

title: Literally Hitler
summary: John critiques the reflexive comparison of political figures to Hitler, tracing 'reductio ad Hitlerum' back to 1951 and noting earlier villains like the Pharaoh of Exodus. He argues such analogies are counterproductive: they don't dissuade supporters, they insult them, and they falsely imply the US is in 1930s-Germany-level peril, when in fact the economy has grown, unemployment and crime have dropped, and insurance coverage is at a decades high. He challenges nostalgia for a nonexistent golden age, condemns name-calling (singling out Trump as the worst offender) for making policy discussion impossible, and calls for civil discourse.

title: On the Banning of Looking for Alaska
summary: John responds to Looking for Alaska being the most challenged book of 2015, banned for offensive language and sexual explicitness. He explains that objections typically stem from one page read out of context: a clinical, deliberately unerotic oral-sex scene contrasted with a later tender, non-explicit kissing scene, the whole point being that emotional intimacy outweighs empty physical contact. He argues teenagers are thoughtful readers who don't read prescriptively, that such decisions belong to trained teachers and librarians rather than him, that public education serves the whole social order, and that books challenge and reflect us rather than corrupt us.

title: Brazil's Government is Falling Apart...and it's Good News?
summary: Hank explains Brazil's political crisis, providing context on the country's size, diversity, inequality, and its relatively peaceful transition from military dictatorship to a young democracy with unusually strong independent courts and police. He traces the rise of Lula and the Workers' Party, their anti-poverty successes, and successor Dilma Rousseff, then details the massive Petrobras corruption scandal, the largest in any democracy, involving construction kickbacks worth billions. He argues the crisis stems from converging factors: the scandal's scale, a deep recession, an unpopular president facing impeachment, a crusading judge (Moro) idolized by the public, and Brazil's bitter partisan divide.

title: Thanks, Lemon
summary: Hank reflects on the death of his greyhound Lemon from bone cancer, admitting that his usual plain, efficient writing style fails him for the things that matter most. He describes disorienting grief where sadness arrives without thought, and insists Lemon was 'just another dog' yet also his treasure who left a hole in his heart. He rejects the pressure to make an outsized mark on the world, reframes their adoption as getting a companion rather than an act of charity, and concludes that feeling valued comes from valuing others, one of the many things his ordinary dog taught him.

title: Is the Gender Pay Gap Real?
summary: John examines the gender pay gap after pushback on his 77-cents claim. He argues the gap is real but complex: the raw full-time gap is 16-21%, shrinking to an 'unexplained' 4-8% attributable to direct discrimination once education, occupation, and hours are controlled. He notes women do more unpaid labor, that mothers face hiring and pay penalties while fathers benefit, and that gaps persist within nearly every profession. Some of it reflects genuine choices, but much stems from discrimination and gendered social expectations we can change.

title: Why the Word "Millennial" Makes me Cringe
summary: Hank pushes back on the word 'millennial,' which he says was coined by baby boomers, not the generation itself, then used to make sweeping generalizations and mock young people as entitled. Opening with a hardworking 23-year-old he met on a flight, he argues millennials face unprecedented pressures and unrealistic expectations. He finds it strange that older generations who worked to make life easier now complain that young people have it too easy, when evidence increasingly shows they don't.

title: Why Are Vegetarians Annoying? (An Exploration of a Cultural Rift)
summary: Hank explores why vegetarians provoke annoyance, beginning by conceding that vegetarians are right: meat causes animal suffering, climate change, deforestation, and inefficient land use. He admits he keeps eating meat anyway because it's easier and he lacks willpower, comparing it to spending on luxuries instead of charity. The real conflict, he argues, is that vegetarians work hard to live by values that clash with a meat-normalized society, and breaking with culture is socially costly. He predicts future generations will find our meat-eating absurd.

title: Benghazi: Explained
summary: Hank explains the 2012 Benghazi attack and its politicization. He recounts the facts: militants attacked the US consulate, killing Ambassador Chris Stevens, Sean Smith, and two former Navy SEALs. He argues the State Department made real, acknowledged mistakes in security but that accusations of criminal negligence or cover-up by Obama and Clinton are unsupported after eight investigations. He cites Kevin McCarthy's admission that the Benghazi committee aimed to hurt Clinton's poll numbers, and notes the attack devastated Benghazi itself, contributing to a second Libyan civil war.

title: Understanding the Refugee Crisis in Europe, Syria, and around the World
summary: John explains the global refugee crisis, centering the drowned toddler Aylan Kurdi to counter the numbing effect of statistics. He traces Syria's civil war under Assad, which displaced millions, with most refugees straining Turkey, Lebanon, and Jordan. He stresses the legal distinction between migrants and refugees, who hold rights under international law, and argues Europe keeps sea routes dangerous to deter arrivals rather than opening legal quotas. He assigns blame widely, including wealthy nations accepting few refugees, and insists all humans are 'our people.'

title: Understanding the Financial Crisis in Greece
summary: John explains Greece's financial crisis. Joining the euro in 2001 meant Greece shared monetary policy but kept its own fiscal policy, and successive governments hid the true scale of deficits until a 2009 revelation of a 13.9% deficit spooked investors and spiked borrowing costs. The troika (EU, ECB, IMF) provided bailouts largely to repay German and French banks, in exchange for austerity that shrank the economy and deepened a depression rivaling America's. In 2015 a new leftist government and referendum rejected further austerity.

title: Why is College So Expensive?
summary: Hank investigates why US college costs have tripled since 1980. He shows instruction spending has merely kept pace with inflation, partly via cheap adjunct labor, so the surge comes from non-academic categories, especially 'student services.' Colleges increasingly act like businesses competing for student-customers with amenities like climbing walls and nice dorms, hiring administrators far faster than professors. He notes college remains a good financial deal, but argues education should be about enrichment rather than maximizing revenue and selling an experience.

title: Is Obamacare Working? The Affordable Care Act Five Years Later
summary: John assesses the Affordable Care Act on its fifth anniversary. He describes the pre-ACA system where 44 million were uninsured and insurers denied coverage for pre-existing conditions, while the US spent over 17% of GDP on healthcare for worse outcomes than peer nations. The ACA created exchanges, expanded Medicaid, and banned discrimination, and after a disastrous rollout now works, insuring over 12 million and freeing the labor market. But he concludes it grew atop the old system without solving long-term cost growth, which would require more radical change.

title: Places I've Never Been
summary: John recounts discovering that a widely shared quote attributed to him, 'I'm in love with cities I've never been to and people I've never met,' is not actually in Paper Towns. He admits he assumed he'd written it without checking, until a Reddit post revealed it was written years earlier by a thirteen-year-old Nerdfighter named Melody, who was receiving hate for supposedly stealing his quote. He arranges to retroactively pay her royalties and uses the mix-up to critique how badly the internet handles credit, sourcing, and copyright.

title: Understanding Boko Haram
summary: John explains Boko Haram by tracing Nigeria's history: Islam spread by trade to the north while Christianity took hold in the south, and British indirect rule left the north poorer and less educated. This impoverished, poorly governed region birthed Boko Haram ('western education is forbidden') under Mohammed Yusuf, who was executed in 2009, after which the group turned violently militant, bombing churches, murdering vaccine workers and students, and kidnapping 276 schoolgirls. John stresses the group was allowed to grow through global inattention, and that 99.9% of Nigerians reject it.

title: Racism in the United States: By the Numbers
summary: John presents data showing systemic racism against African Americans is not debatable, despite most white Americans denying it. In criminal justice, Black men receive 20% longer sentences and are arrested for drugs at three times the rate despite equal use. In employment, identical resumes with names like Lakisha or Jamal got 50% fewer callbacks than Emily or Brendan. He cites disparities in education, healthcare, and inherited wealth rooted in slavery, acknowledges correlation isn't causation, and urges listening to affected people, not just statistics.

title: Hong Kong Protests Explained
summary: Hank explains the 2014 Hong Kong protests, tracing how the city was ceded to Britain, leased for 99 years, and returned to China in 1997 under 'one country, two systems,' keeping its own currency, courts, and capitalist economy. China needs Hong Kong's freedom for its economic value yet fears any appearance of weakness or instability. Protests erupted because Beijing would only allow elections from pre-approved candidates, and Hank notes Hong Kongers, long able to protest but never to elect leaders, found the harsh police response especially alarming.

title: Deserving
summary: John reflects on luck and deserving, saying the success of The Fault in Our Stars film came not from his merit but from fortune, being born male, white, in a rich country, writing in English, and having Hank as a collaborator. He warns that believing you deserve good things breeds a self-centered blindness to inequality, and that it logically implies deserving bad things too, which no one does, whether illness, pain, or poverty. Invoking Big History, he concludes value isn't earned by luck and that gratitude and working together are the proper response to being alive.

title: Hank vs. Hank: The Net Neutrality Debate in 3 Minutes
summary: Hank stages a debate between himself as an internet user and himself as a cable company to explain net neutrality, the principle that ISPs must deliver all content at equal speed. The cable-company Hank argues bandwidth hogs like Netflix should pay for fast lanes, while the user Hank counters that speeding some up necessarily slows others, destroys competition, and citing Comcast throttling Netflix shows the harm is real. He urges viewers to tell the FCC to classify broadband as a common carrier and to support pro-neutrality organizations.

title: What Boys Look For in Girls
summary: Hank subverts the 'what boys look for in girls' video genre by cataloging wildly different beauty standards across cultures, from lip plaques and neck rings to fat camps and sharpened teeth, to show that human mate selection is essentially arbitrary. Therefore, he argues, basing self-worth on being found attractive is foolish, though it burdens women more since culture ties female value to beauty in ways it doesn't for men. He affirms that self-expression through appearance is fine, but that you exist for your own sake, not to please anyone else.

title: Mass Incarceration in the US
summary: Hank argues that America's incarceration system fails at its most important goal, correction, while excelling only at punishment. He cites that the US holds 25% of the world's prisoners with a rate that rose over 400% in 30 years, and points to solitary confinement (deemed torture), unregulated punishment with no appeals, and post-conviction barriers to jobs, welfare, and housing that drive recidivism, homelessness, and suicide. He concludes that being tough on crime got conflated with being tough on criminals, making mass incarceration a cruel, costly $75 billion failed experiment.

title: Is the American Dream Real?
summary: John opens with an anecdote of a Craigslist mover who rose in publishing, embodying the American Dream, then argues the data contradict the myth. He notes 42% of American men born in the bottom income fifth stay there (versus 25-30% in Denmark and the UK), that birth wealth predicts outcomes more here than in other developed nations, and that US women earn only 77 cents per male dollar. He warns that systemic inequality of opportunity threatens future innovation and could make the US less appealing to talented people, harming all Americans.

title: Sexual Abuse, Consent, and Culture
summary: Hank argues that cultural silence and metaphor around sex leave norms dangerously unspoken, and that framing romance as a predator/prey pursuit, the 'cruise missile' chasing the 'kitten', enables abuse by assuming resistance regardless of desire. He defines sexual abuse as coercion into unwanted sexual activity and stresses that consent, not the absence of 'no', is essential. He announces concrete steps: a nerdfighter task force, funded videos on consent and abuse, and partnerships with sexual assault organizations.

title: Understanding Ukraine: The Problems Today and Some Historical Context
summary: John gives historical context for the 2014 Ukraine crisis, tracing the Crimean War, brief post-WWI independence, Stalin's engineered famine that killed millions and his deportation of Crimean Tatars, and Khrushchev's 1954 transfer of Crimea to Ukraine. He walks through the modern demographic split and political tug-of-war from the Orange Revolution to Yanukovych's 2013 pivot toward Russia, the deadly Maidan protests, and Russia's invasion of Crimea. He presents two competing narratives and argues Ukraine, the 'borderland,' needs decades of stability to define its own identity.

title: A Middle Aged Man
summary: John reflects on realizing he is genuinely middle-aged, joking that if tripling your age yields a reasonable lifespan you're not there yet. Admitting he has never knowingly heard a Justin Bieber song, he listens to several and likes them, then pivots to his real point: despite his total ignorance of Bieber, he knows the color of the Lamborghini from his DUI arrest. He argues that culture elevates celebrities so everyone can relish their downfalls, that this public chastising helps no one and may harm us, and that choosing what we pay attention to matters.

title: Should We Raise the Minimum Wage?
summary: John examines whether raising the minimum wage helps or hurts, laying out the Econ 101 case that price floors reduce growth and jobs, then countering it with the Card and Krueger New Jersey-Pennsylvania study showing employment actually rose. He explains raises get spent, boosting activity, and reduce costly turnover, though prices rise modestly. Citing evidence that a 10% hike cuts poverty about 2.5%, he calls it neither a job-killer nor an inequality cure but the 'cheapest lunch' for reducing poverty.

title: GOOD NEWS: 14 Reasons 2014 May Be the Best Year Ever
summary: John counters pessimism with data-driven good news heading into 2014. He cites falling US divorce rates, declining unintended pregnancies and infant mortality (which stabilizes population as families shrink), and a dramatic global drop in absolute poverty from 43% to 20% since 1990. He adds falling cancer mortality, faster internet, cheaper safer air and car travel, fewer euthanized pets, progress against malaria, and rising female literacy, arguing this progress, though tenuous and partly unsustainable, is real and worth celebrating.

title: How to Apologize like a Fartbag
summary: Hank offers a guide to genuine apology, defining a 'fartbag' as someone who hurts a person and then blames them for their pain. His dos and don'ts: feel bad (that's a conscience), figure out what you did wrong and why, provide context without making excuses, accept blame rather than merely expressing sympathy, and don't treat apologizing as asking forgiveness or losing a zero-sum battle. He cites research that apologizers feel weaker but are seen as stronger, and insists the final requirement is to actually change, or you're just an eloquent lying fartbag.

title: On Middle School Misery
summary: Prompted by a message from a middle schooler at his old school, an exhausted John recalls being a miserable, bullied 'stupid nerd' who fantasized about revenge. He reflects that his bullies weren't evil but kids dealing with their own fear and pain, that their cruelty said nothing about his worth, and that many people's kindness helped him survive. Rejecting Lord of the Flies pessimism about adolescents, he argues kids are capable of great kindness, and closes with Robert Frost's 'the only way out is through,' reassuring young viewers that middle school need not be life's best years.

title: Perspective
summary: Responding to Hank's video on perspective, John tells the story of being 24, depressed, and dumped in Chicago, subsisting on Sprite until his parents brought him home to therapy and better medication. He credits a nudge from his boss Bill Ott to watch the film Harvey, whose line 'you must be oh so smart or oh so pleasant, I recommend pleasant', helped him feel less hopeless and led to the pages that became Looking for Alaska. His thesis: you can't know what an experience will mean until you become your future self, and gaining that perspective takes millions of seconds only time can buy.

title: Applying for Health Insurance: Then and Now
summary: John applies for health insurance twice to compare the new Affordable Care Act exchange with the old private-insurance process. The ACA site is glitchy, throwing repeated server errors before letting him create an account, but once in, the questions are simple and it only asks whether he smokes. The old private application, by contrast, runs 25 pages and demands a decade of intrusive medical history, doctor phone numbers, and test results. Despite the buggy servers, he concludes the ACA process was more than twice as fast and far less confusing and invasive than buying insurance the old way.

title: The Lowdown on the Shutdown
summary: Hank explains the 2013 government shutdown, describing how Congress must approve a budget or pass a continuing resolution to keep spending legal. He recounts how the Republican House attached a one-year delay of Obamacare while the Democratic Senate refused, causing the shutdown, though Obamacare is funded separately and took effect anyway. He identifies gerrymandered districts that reward uncooperative extremes as the deeper cause, notes which services close versus continue, and says roughly 800,000 workers are furloughed but the crisis will be resolved.

title: Invalid Arguments: Climate Change
summary: Hank rebuts the top ten arguments used to deny human-caused climate change. He counters that past natural warming was often amplified by CO2 (90% followed CO2 rises), that warming shows up across cities, rural stations, satellites, and ocean thermometers, and that short flat trends are meaningless since climate is measured over decades. He addresses the 1970s ice-age myth and Antarctic sea ice versus melting land ice, and while calling fossil fuels miraculous, argues their costs require using less and developing alternatives, expressing faith we can solve the problem once we agree one exists.

title: Syria in Five Minutes
summary: Hank explains the Syrian civil war, starting with arbitrary colonial borders enclosing diverse groups and Cold War ties that put the Alawite Ba'ath Party, backed by Russia (which needs Syria's warm-water ports), in power under Assad. He describes a repressed Sunni majority whose Arab Spring protests were met with jail, killing, and torture, spawning the Free Syrian Army and rival factions. He covers Obama's 'red line,' Assad's confirmed sarin use, and Kerry's accidental chemical-weapons deal, concluding no one truly knows the right action but everyone can help by donating to UN refugee relief.

title: Why Are American Health Care Costs So High?
summary: John investigates why US health care is so expensive, revealing that Americans pay more tax per capita on health care than Britons yet most get no public coverage, with total spending near 18% of GDP versus 9% in Australia. He debunks blaming overutilization, obesity, and malpractice (tort reform cut costs only 0.1%), showing instead that everything simply costs more. The core cause is the lack of centralized price negotiation: unlike the UK's NHS leveraging huge contracts, fragmented US buyers face 'inelastic demand,' so providers charge what they want while outcomes stay unexceptional.

title: Egypt....Explained!
summary: Hank breaks down Egypt's 2013 upheaval by identifying four power blocs: the old Nasser-era regime, the Muslim Brotherhood, the military, and disenfranchised young activists. He emphasizes that Egypt's military is unusual because it controls roughly 40% of the economy, so it seeks stability over a formal power grab. He traces how Mubarak alienated all sides, how post-revolution elections handed Islamists power, and how President Morsi's overreach triggered massive protests that led the military to remove him in an unusually popular coup.

title: Inside North Korea
summary: John discusses 'Escape from Camp 14,' the story of Shin Dong-hyuk, born inside a North Korean prison camp who escaped. He argues the book's power lies not in an indomitable human spirit but in its fragility: Shin informed on his own mother and brother's escape plan, leading to their execution, because that was the only morality he knew. John contends that a moral center is not innate but developed, and that Shin only became fully human after escaping and learning ethics elsewhere.

title: North Korea: Explained
summary: Using Google autocomplete questions, Hank explains North Korea's 2013 saber-rattling. He argues its nuclear threats are longstanding bluster driven by UN sanctions, a new South Korean president, US-South Korea military exercises, and opaque internal politics. He stresses the regime's real victims are its own malnourished people, noting entire families are imprisoned for generations. He explains the dictatorship needs the US as an enemy to appear powerful, and that appeasing its tantrums only encourages more.

title: Doing Things, On the Importance of
summary: Reflecting on a big week, John admits his instinct after success is anxiety and retreating to bed, which he calls a bad strategy. Instead he lists twelve ongoing projects he and Hank are pursuing, from Mental Floss's new channel and CrashCourse to DFTBA Records, VidCon, and the Kiva nerdfighters nearing two million dollars in loans. His underlying point is that Hank's constant flow of ideas pulls him out of self-absorption and forces him to keep working.

title: Why Does Congress Suck?
summary: John explains why Congress has become so ineffective, noting the recent Congress passed only 219 bills, mostly naming buildings. His central argument is gerrymandering: state legislatures draw districts to be safe 'landslide districts,' collapsing the number of competitive swing seats. Because representatives fear only primary challengers accusing them of insufficient purity, they have no incentive to compromise. He warns that while Congress was designed to be limited, losing the ability to govern would be worse than any fiscal cliff.

title: The 51st State
summary: Hank argues the overlooked story of the 2012 election was Puerto Rico voting for the first time to favor statehood. He explains Puerto Ricans are US citizens who obey US laws and serve in the military but cannot vote federally, effectively a colony the UN frowns upon. He lays out the obstacles: added Medicare costs, the need for a two-thirds vote, shifting congressional balance likely toward Democrats, and Americans' deep resistance to changing the seemingly fixed number of 50 states.

title: Understanding the National Debt and Budget Deficit
summary: John distinguishes debt (total liabilities) from deficit (yearly shortfall) and argues the US national debt, while huge, is not currently a crisis. He notes most debt is owed to Americans themselves, that it is extremely cheap to service, and that China cannot call it in early. He explains governments, like businesses, sometimes should run deficits, especially in recessions. The real danger is future: if the US lost its status as the safest place for money, spiraling interest rates could bring a Greece-like crisis.

title: Human Sexuality is Complicated...
summary: Hank argues human sexuality cannot be sorted into two neat boxes but exists on infinite continuums. He separates several independent spectrums: biological sex, gender identity, sexual orientation (including asexuality), the newer concept of romantic orientation, sexual behavior versus orientation, and socially constructed gender roles. He stresses each dimension varies in intensity and can shift over time, and that gender, not biological sex, should determine pronouns. His goal is that understanding this complexity reduces hate and self-hate.

title: What To Do With Your Life
summary: Answering a college freshman's question about what to do with life, John rejects the premise that adulthood is a single fixed path. He argues people wrongly imagine they must pick one thing, when in reality life keeps offering new directions and decisions get unmade by circumstance. Citing figures like poet-doctor William Carlos Williams and chef-spy Julia Child, he advises studying broadly without fear. Ultimately, he says what you do matters less than who you do it with.

title: Why The Rich Pay Lower Taxes
summary: Hank explains why Warren Buffett can pay a lower tax rate than his secretary. He clarifies that ordinary income tax is progressive and bracketed, so moving up only taxes the additional income at higher rates. Capital gains, however, how the wealthy make most of their money, are taxed at a flat 15%. He questions the rationale that investment deserves lower taxes than earned income, noting weak supporting data, and calls the arrangement bad ethics even if he is open to being proven wrong on policy.

title: Is College Worth It?
summary: John tackles whether college is worth its cost. Running a rough calculation of tuition, loan interest, and opportunity cost against lifetime earnings, he concludes that despite being criminally overpriced, an accredited college degree still typically pays for itself. But his larger point is that treating life as purely economic misses the value. Recalling cleaning vomit at Steak and Shake versus a lower-paying but fulfilling job at a magazine, he argues education's real gift is perspective and a more informed, fulfilling life.

title: An Open Letter to Students Returning to School
summary: In an open letter to returning students, John notes compulsory education is only about 250 years old, a period coinciding with enormous human progress like antibiotics and Mars landings. He acknowledges school can feel boring and teachers imperfect, joking that everyone is stupid but learning to be less stupid together. His key argument reframes education as not for the student's benefit alone: society invests in students because a well-educated population makes everyone better off, so students are on a special mission.

title: Religion and Gay Marriage
summary: John examines gay marriage from a religious angle by questioning what marriage even is. He shows 'traditional marriage' is a myth: biblical figures had many wives, church weddings and clergy were uncommon before the 16th century, and intersex people have always married. He acknowledges some religions view marriage as a covenant with God, but argues basing legal marriage rights on such covenants creates impossible problems. Marriage, he concludes, is defined by the people inside it, and denying it is discrimination.

title: Your Arguments Are Invalid: Gay Marriage
summary: Reacting to North Carolina's constitutional ban on gay marriage, Hank dismantles the arguments against it. He says religious objections amount to oppression given the separation of church and state, and coins 'hypothetheory' for the untested claim that gay marriage undermines the institution of marriage. He argues the real objection is that it feels unnatural, but insists complexity, not rigid categories, is the most natural thing in the world. The only argument that matters, he says, is that all citizens deserve equality under the law.

title: F#*%? An Explanation of Curse Words
summary: Hank explores what makes swear words special. He defines a curse word as one you know not to say in front of your mom, distinct from a slur that targets a group. Citing research that swearing increases pain tolerance, he explains profanity carries real power and is processed in the brain's emotional limbic system rather than the language centers. He suggests swearing may even hint at language's emotional origins, and closes on the idea that words are a kind of magic that can build or tear down worlds.

title: Top 5 Reasons ████ ██ ██████
summary: Hank demonstrates Fair Use by commenting on a copyrighted song, then argues that while copyright law is necessary, pending legislation like the PROTECT IP Act goes too far. His top-five list contends the bills would criminalize ordinary internet users, stifle creativity and economic growth (citing Machinima as tolerated infringement), wrongly hold sites like Tumblr liable for user uploads, and let corporations get videos taken down with no human review. He concludes that the internet's creative explosion is a rare economic engine that shouldn't be broken.

title: Coming Out and Sperm Spikes
summary: Hank uses the wave-particle duality of electrons as a metaphor for the difficulty of categorizing people. For National Coming Out Day, he recounts that as a boy he feared he was gay because he matched stereotypical 'social cues' like ice skating to the Village People, which terrified him. He explains he's actually a mostly-heterosexual, somewhat effeminate 'less guyish guy,' arguing sexual orientation is rarely 100% either way. His message: be honest with yourself and others even when you're unsure who you are.

title: The Great Gatsby: Living the Dream in the Valley of Ashes
summary: John walks through the themes and symbolism of The Great Gatsby, spotlighting the Valley of Ashes and the all-seeing eyes of Dr. T.J. Eckleburg. He argues characters needn't be likable, that gold/yellow functions as an 'enchanted' symbolic color, and that Gatsby's wealth is a means to win Daisy and reclaim a pure past. He reads the novel as an indictment of the American Dream: we always want more because we really want to return to a lost golden age, a pursuit that trails 'foul dust' of violence and corruption.

title: How to Become An Adult
summary: John advises new graduates entering the 'real world,' which he says is really a 'CMAPT world' of crushing monotony and paralyzing terror. He insists adulthood genuinely gets better, and offers two remedies: time, and finding an 'Ilene'—a mentor doing what you want to do, better than you can. He recounts how editor Ilene Cooper spent two years helping him turn 40 pages into Looking For Alaska, crediting her for his writing career and urging viewers to seek out such a person.

title: Understanding America's Debt Problem
summary: John explains the supposed American debt crisis, defining unsecured versus collateralized debt, GDP, and credit ratings, and notes the US debt-to-GDP ratio hit roughly 100%. He observes that although governments above 90% often default and S&P downgraded US debt to AA+, US borrowing costs actually fell. He attributes this to global volatility making US debt still the safest option, the world's investment in the dollar, and the fact that all currency is ultimately backed only by trust—which dysfunction slowly erodes.

title: I Am Not a P#$$Y - this video contains mild swear words.
summary: Hank responds to a driver who called him a slur from a truck while he was cycling. He argues that riding a bike among cars is an act of trust and bravery, whereas taunting a vulnerable cyclist from inside 3,000 pounds of steel is actually cowardice—bravery is not strength against a lesser foe. Reflecting on being bullied as a kid, he offers two lessons: it isn't about you, it's about the person doing it, and your job isn't to 'deal with' it but to survive it, because it will end and then get better.

title: Your Yard Is EVIL
summary: Sweltering in a heat wave, John rails against American front lawns as a prime example of status quo bias run amok. He notes turf grass is the most irrigated US crop, consuming about 30% of drinking water (4 billion gallons daily) and 70 million pounds of pesticides yearly, all for lawns no one uses and that don't feed anyone. He blames British inheritance, imagines a world where vegetable gardens are the norm, and notes that status quo bias ironically hasn't preserved lawns—it's made them ever bigger.

title: Kill the Dollar Bill!
summary: John explains the 'denomination effect'—people spend money more readily in small denominations and as coins rather than bills. He renews his crusade against the penny (worthless, costs more than a cent to make) and argues for killing the one-dollar bill: a GAO report estimated replacing it with dollar coins would save $5.5 billion over 30 years, since bills last only 40 months versus decades for coins, and a billion unused dollar coins already exist. He notes this spending boost would even aid economic growth.

title: John Green is an IDIOT! And so are you.
summary: Hank argues that all humans are 'stupid idiots' largely because of cognitive bias, which exists to speed decision-making but leads us astray when unexamined. He walks through several types: the blind-spot bias (thinking others are more biased than we are), anchoring bias (fixating on one piece of info, as in the spun-wheel Africa study), confirmation bias, authority and egocentric bias, and hyperbolic discounting. He praises science as a rare institution built to eliminate bias and recommends reading about it, citing Michael Shermer's The Believing Brain.

title: How to Turn Your Dumb Summer Job Into $162,000
summary: For Independence Day, John gives financial-independence advice, showing how a crappy summer job can become a fortune through an IRA and compound interest. Using ~6.5% real global growth, he calculates that saving ~$1,970 a year from age 16-19 could grow to over $162,000 by retirement, effectively turning $7.50/hour into $125/hour. His key lesson: saving early matters far more than saving a lot, since a 33-year-old's $2,000 grows to only ~$20,000—so ordinary people can use the same wealth-building tools as the rich.

title: Do You Believe in God?
summary: Hank declines to directly answer whether he believes in God, arguing the question's terms are ill-defined: everyone pictures 'God' differently, and 'belief' versus 'knowledge' is the entire branch of philosophy called epistemology. He probes why people ask, identifying two motives—seeking an authority to inform their own uncertain views (which he says they must resolve themselves) and using a one-word answer as a shortcut to categorize him. He calls that pattern-matching dangerous when applied to people, joking he's a unicorn-blood-drinking Satanist.

title: On Religion
summary: Drawing on his past as a hospital chaplain and near-enrollment in divinity school, John argues that although most people live in 'functional nihilism'—wasting their remarkable minds and bodies on distraction—no one is truly a nihilist, because everyone feels called to make their life matter. He says this call to meaning underlies both religious and secular worldviews, so debating God's mere existence is a way of dodging the deeper question of how we'll create meaning. Like fleeing a burning house, the response matters more than the voice's source.

title: Baby on the Road: Thoughts from Places
summary: In a 'Thoughts from Places' video, John narrates a speed-focused family drive from Birmingham to Indianapolis with his wife and young sons. He reflects nostalgically on his youthful westward road trips to places like the Grand Canyon and Nebraska's tiny courthouse, quoting Robert Penn Warren's 'All the King's Men' on west as where you flee. Contemplating becoming a capital-A Adult with responsibilities, he concludes that while people say the journey matters more than the destination, this trip's destination—home, with his whole family—is one his old westward trips never reached.

title: Probability 101
summary: Hank teaches introductory probability, having viewers flip coins to show that random individual events produce predictable aggregate patterns—of 102,400 flippers, about 100 will get ten heads, so a 'magic' streak means nothing. He defines P(A) as favorable over total outcomes, the complement 1−P(A), multiplying independent probabilities for AND events, and adding for OR events, illustrating with coins, dice, and poker hands. He tips viewers to convert OR problems into AND problems using complements, then applies it to darkly humorous life-or-death word problems.

title: God and Grain: The French Revolution, Part I
summary: John narrates the opening of the French Revolution, setting the stage with Enlightenment thinkers challenging divine-right monarchy, France's crushing debt (50% of its budget), and a 1789 bread famine. He explains the Estates General, the disenfranchised Third Estate declaring itself the National Assembly and swearing the Tennis Court Oath, and the storming of the Bastille (mainly for guns). He covers the Declaration of the Rights of Man and the Women's March that forced Louis XVI to Paris, leaving France leaderless and armed—foreshadowing worse to come.

title: Is Obama a Muslim? Examine the Evidence!
summary: Responding to a poll showing over 20% of Americans think Obama is Muslim, John humorously tests the claim against the Five Pillars of Islam. He shows Obama fails to observe prayer, zakat (giving under 1% to charity), and fasting (eating publicly during Ramadan), and cannot make the Hajj since Saudi Arabia bars non-Muslims from Mecca. He concludes Obama is either not a Muslim or a spectacularly bad one, and urges the media to move on to real topics.

title: The Greek Debt Crisis Explained in Four Minutes
summary: John explains the Greek debt crisis, noting sovereign debt is not inherently bad and isn't a partisan issue. Wealthy 'fancy pants' countries can borrow cheaply because markets assume they'll repay, and running deficits can make sense when growth outpaces cheap debt, as after 1945. The crisis began when markets doubted Greece's ability to repay, raising its borrowing costs in a vicious circle. He warns defaults could spread and stresses the need to restore confident lending, as the EU and IMF attempt a bailout.

title: I Shall BLOW YOUR MIND
summary: Hank attempts to blow John's mind with the scale of the universe, escalating from Earth to the Sun (a million Earths could fit inside) to the 200-400 billion stars in our galaxy. The climax is the Hubble Ultra-Deep Field: Hubble was pointed at a tiny, seemingly empty black patch of sky, and the days-long exposure revealed over 3,000 galaxies, each with billions of stars. Hank concludes that humanity's capacity to understand the universe is more important than the star stuff itself.

title: Health Care Overhaul Summarized Via MASSIVE PIG
summary: Using the world's largest boar, 'Walkin' Tall,' who is too big to walk, as a metaphor for America's healthcare system, John lays out the debate in nonpartisan terms. He notes bipartisan agreement that the US overspends and that coverage is unfair, then explains the public option, nonprofit competitor plans, and the status quo. He argues America already has inefficient socialized medicine since hospitals treat the uninsured, and concludes the real question is whether healthcare is a privilege or a right, arguing it is a right the government must protect.

title: On Iran, Islam, and Amazingly Stupid Comments
summary: Prompted by hostile YouTube comments on his earlier Iran video, John rebuts several arguments. He counters that caring about Iran and America aren't mutually exclusive on a shared planet, that Iran's president matters (Ahmadinejad wrecked the economy and unleashed morality police), and most centrally that Islam is not monolithic. He argues all religions are diverse responses to revelation, noting both protesters and their oppressors are inspired by Islam, and that people, not revelation, choose to make the world better or worse.

title: Sarah Palin: An Introduction
summary: John introduces newly named Republican VP nominee Sarah Palin for viewers who avoid the news. He lists key facts: she is Alaska's governor of under two years, reliably conservative on social issues (pro-life, anti-gay-marriage, NRA moose hunter), has an anti-corruption reputation in a corrupt state, and a son named Track heading to Iraq. He flags her total lack of foreign policy experience as a liability given McCain's age, and notes she's been an Alaska-focused governor rather than a national leader.

title: I Am Not A Pornographer
summary: John defends his novel Looking for Alaska after some people, whose own kids weren't affected, tried to ban it as pornographic despite a school's opt-out permission-slip system. He argues the book cannot be pornographic since its frank sex scene is deliberately awkward and unerotic, written to contrast empty physical intimacy against a later scene of deep emotional connection, arguing against vapid physical interactions. He rejects claims teens can't read critically and condemns organized book-banners, urging supporters to attend the school board meeting.
